that date the deadline for submitting the claims would be midnight on Sunday 7January
2024. 

 33. 
 The test I must apply is whether her illnesses made
her incapable of completing a Claim Form. A Claim Form only requires basic
information; boxes to be ticked identifying the nature of the claims being made
and a very brief overview of the claims. There is no need to submit documents.
Ms Marasteanu was on the point of submitting a complaint to her employer in
November 2023. She says in her email dated 6 November 2023 that she is in the
process of finalising her complaints. She refers in her email to the unexpected
news from the scan and asks for additional time. I accept that this was a very
difficult time for Ms Marasteanu. She was suffering from depression and
anxiety, pelvic pain and going through a difficult pregnancy. I am not
satisfied that despite all these problems she was not capable to completing a
Claim Form. In her witness statement she says that after her suspension in
November 2023 she decided to postpone raising formal concerns indefinitely
until she considered that her and her baby were safe to do so. However, this
does not satisfy the test of being incapable of completing the Claim Form. I
therefore find claims C to H to be time barred. 

 34. In case I am wrong
on this I have also considered whether the submission on 14 February 2025 was
within a reasonable period. If Ms Marasteanu was incapable by reason of
ill-health from submitting her Claim Form before 7 January 2024, she arguably would
have remained incapable until at least the end of her paid maternity leave in early
March 2024. However, there came a point after that when her health problems and
the difficulties associated with looking after Mr Gray decreased. Mr Gray was
walking unaided by 25 November 2024. If I take that as the earliest point at
which she became capable of putting in a Claim Form she should have acted
quickly after that. However, she did not take any steps to progress matters. Her
first meeting with JACS was on 28 January 2025 more than two months later. By
this time the claims were more than a year old. If she wanted to pursue these
claims she should have taken urgent action after 25 November 2024 and submitted
the claims shortly afterwards. She did not do so and therefore the claims were
not submitted within a reasonable period as required by Article 37(2)(b)(ii). 

 35. Claim B is the
poor performance review which was conducted on 18 January 2024 without her
input or knowledge. It is not reasonably practicable for a claimant to submit a
claim about something they have no factual knowledge of and could not
reasonably have found out about. 

 36. Les Amis said at
the Interim Hearing that the HR system which includes the appraisal system
generates an automatic email every time an appraisal is updated. Les Amis were
unable to produce a copy of that email and Ms Marasteanu said that she never
received an email. I accept her evidence and find that she was unaware of the
performance review when it was done in January 2024. I therefore find that it
was not reasonably practicable for her to file her claim within the eight-week
time limit. 

 37. 
 Ms Marasteanu said at the Interim Hearing that she
first discovered the performance review at the end of January 2025 when she
logged onto the HR system to look for evidence for her claims. A print-out from
the Les Amis HR system shows that Ms Marasteanu logged onto the system on 3,
13, 23 and 31 January 2025. In an email sent to the Tribunal after the hearing
Ms Marasteanu confirmed that she
accessed the end of year review on 13 January 2025. 

 38. 
 The claim was filed on 14
February 2025 just over a month after she discovered the report. I do not
consider a delay of a month to be a reasonable period. Upon finding the report
she should have filed the claim within days not waited another month. As the
claim was not presented within a further period that I consider reasonable the
claim is time barred and is struck out. 

 39. Claim A relates to
the payment of a bonus on 20 December 2024. This claim was filed one day late.
I find that it was reasonably practical for Ms Marasteanu to file the claim in
time. I do so for two reasons. First, the fact that accrual of the bonus was
frozen during any period of unpaid leave was communicated to Ms Marasteanu via
email on 6 December 2024. The bonus itself was paid on 20 December 2024. She
had ample time from then to file the Claim Form by midnight on 13 February
2025. She had a meeting with JACS on 28 January 2025 and email communication on
11 February 2025. There was nothing preventing her from filing the Claim Form
on 12 or 13 February 2025. 

 40. Second, she was
wrongly advised by JACS that the last day for filing the claim was 14 February
2025. In Skrzat the Tribunal held that negligent action by a lawyer did
not make it impractical for the claimant to file on time. The service that was
provided by JACS was not dissimilar to the advice that might be provided by a
lawyer. Ms Marasteanu was given wording to put on the Claim Form and the
Adviser checked the draft Form for her. Her position was not materially
different from that of a claimant wrongly advised of the limitation period by a
lawyer. The one difference is that wrong advice from a retained lawyer might
give rise to a negligence claim against the lawyer. Advice provided by JACS has
partial immunity under Article 15 of Schedule 1 of the Jersey Advisory and Conciliation
(Jersey) Law 2003 . However, the lack of any effective redress against an
adviser will be a common situation in Jersey where many advisers do not carry
insurance. I do not consider that the lack of redress against JACS means that a
JACS adviser should be treated differently from a lawyer. 

 41. As it was
reasonably practical to file Claim A on time Ms Marasteanu is not entitled to
an extension. The claim is out of time and is struck out. 

 42. Had I found that
one or more of the claims was in time I would then have had to consider whether
any of the earlier discrimination claims were part of a continuing act under
Article 37(3)(a) of the Discrimination Law. As I have found that none of the claims were
submitted in time, I do not need to do this exercise. 

 Conclusion 

 43. All Ms
Marasteanu’s claims under the Discrimination Law and her single claim under the
Employment Law relating to ante-natal appointments were filed out of time and
are dismissed. 

 Advocate Dr Elena Moran, Chairman 
 Date: 31 July 2025 

 Page Last Updated: 07 Aug 2025

title: BETWEEN DANIEL MORGAN v DISCRIMINATION TRIBUNAL IN THE MATTER BETWEEN DANIEL MORGAN CLAIMANT AND R P H LIMITED T/A FLORIDA POOLS & SPAS
text:
IN THE JERSEY EMPLOYMENT AND
DISCRIMINATION TRIBUNAL 

 IN
THE MATTER BETWEEN 

 DANIEL
 MORGAN 

 CLAIMANT 

 AND 

 R P H LIMITED 

 T/A FLORIDA POOLS & SPAS 

 RESPONDENTS 

TRIBUNAL JUDGMENT 

 Reference: [2025]
TRE 031 

 Date
of Hearing: 6 August 2025 

 Before:
 Mrs
H Westmacott, Deputy Chairman 

 For
the Claimant: In
person 

 For the Respondent: Advocate O
Blakeley, Blakeley Legal 

 DECISION 

 The claim for constructive
unfair dismissal succeeds and the Respondent shall pay £4,712 in compensation
to the Claimant. The claim for unpaid wages fails. 

REASONS 

 Introduction 

 1. The
Claimant was employed by the Respondent from 30 January 2024 to 27 February
2025 as a pool
service engineer. 

 2. The
Claimant issued a claim form with the tribunal on 3 March 2025 claiming constructive
unfair dismissal and unpaid wages against the Respondent. By a response form
presented on 27 March 2025, the Respondent denied the claims. 

 3. A
case management hearing was held on 12 May 2025, at which a final hearing was
listed to determine the claims (the “Hearing”). This judgment records my
decision together with the reasons for it, following that Hearing. 

 4. In
advance of the Hearing I was provided with a file containing the documents the
parties wished to put before me in support of the claim and the response (the
“File”). The numbers in square brackets below are references to the relevant
page numbers of the File. 

 5. At
the outset of the Hearing the Respondent indicated it did not agree to the
documents at pages 132 to 138 being included in the File on the basis they were
not relevant to the claims or response. I agree that they are not relevant and
I did not consider them when reaching my decision on the claims. 

 6. In
advance of the Hearing, I was also provided with written statements from the Claimant
and, on behalf of the Respondent, from Alan Corke (General Manager), Shane
Turrell (Managing Director), Georgie Mason (Office / Accounts Manager) and Emma
Terry (Managing Director), all of whom attended the Hearing and responded to questions
put to them during the Hearing. 

 Unfair Dismissal Claim 

 Evidence 

 7. On
26 January 2024 the Claimant signed a contract with the Respondent stating that
he would have no guaranteed hours of work and would only be paid for the hours he
worked [30-32]. 

 April
2024 

 8. The
parties agree that in April 2024 the Claimant’s contract changed so that he was
contracted to work at least 38 hours per week (the “38 Hours Term”) and that,
although no new contract was signed, this new term was agreed orally and
impliedly by the parties conduct, in that the Claimant continued to work for
the Respondent and the Respondent continued to pay him for that work on the
basis of this verbal agreement. 

 9. Ms
Terry states that during a meeting with the Claimant on 24 April 2024 he was
presented with a new employment contract. A copy of this has not been provided
to the Tribunal, but paragraph 8 of Ms Terry’s statement states: 

 “ This
document reflected the proposed change from a zero-hour arrangement to a full-time
position, outlining the new terms and conditions of [the Claimant’s] employment.
The entirety of the new contract terms, including working hours, remuneration,
and entitlements, were explained to him in detail. ” 

 10. At
the Hearing Ms Terry clarified that while she did meet with the Claimant on
this date, she did not provide the Claimant with a copy of the new contract nor
explain its terms to him. She also does not specifically recall anyone else
doing this, but she said it would have happened as this is what either Ms Mason
or another employee would have done. Ms Mason’s statement does not refer to
doing this nor do any other witnesses’ statements. 

 11. At
the Hearing the Claimant said he did not recall being offered a contract to
sign at this time. He does recall talking to Ms Terry about getting a new
written contract, but does not recall ever receiving one. 

 November
2024 

 12. The
Respondent asserts that, apart from when the Claimant took leave, he always worked
at least 38 hours per week after it was agreed that he would be contracted to do
so, until his employment terminated [21]. 

 13. The
Claimant has not put forward evidence to show that this was not the case, save
that he states in his witness statement that in November 2024 he was only paid
around £900. At the Hearing he mentioned that he took some leave that month. He
did not state whether this was paid or unpaid leave and he did not specify why
his wages did not equate to what he should have been paid had he worked 38
hours per week for the month. 

 14. None
of the Resondent’s witnesses were able to comment on what happened in November
2024 when asked about this at the Hearing and a copy of the pay statement for
that month was not included in the File. 

 January
2025 

 15. The
Claimant asserts that in January 2025 he asked Ms Mason about the amount of his
wages and questioned his holiday pay, as he was concerned because he was being
paid different amounts each month. In his witness statement he said he was
worried because there was little work over the winter, he had witnessed financial

 Relevant
Law 

 35. The
recent tribunal judgment in the case of Rasdall v TP Global Finance
Limited [2024] TRE 238 sets out the law on constructive dismissal as it
has been summarised many times in earlier tribunal judgments. In addition, it
makes references to a number of other UK cases which are helpful to consider
when deciding whether there was constructive dismissal in this case. Paragraphs
4 to 9 of the judgment in Rasdall state: 

 “4. The law on constructive unfair dismissal is
summarised in paragraphs 19 to 26 of Dunham v Lightening Hotels Limited [2021]
TRE 55. 

 19. Article 62 EJL sets out the
circumstances in which an employee is dismissed including the following, which
is commonly known as constructive dismissal: 

 “62 Circumstances in which an employee is
dismissed 

 (1) For the purposes of this Part an employee
is dismissed by his or her employer if ... 

 (c) the employee terminates the contract
under which he or she is employed (with or without notice) in circumstances in
which the employee is entitled to terminate it without notice by reason of the
employer's conduct.” 

 20. It is well established under case law
that the conditions which must be met to prove constructive dismissal
are: 

 (a) the employer must be in breach of either
an express or an implied contractual term; 

 (b) the
breach must be fundamental, amounting to a repudiatory breach; 

 (c) the employee must resign, at least in
part, in response to the breach; and 

 (d) the
employee must not have waived the breach and affirmed the contract. 

 21. In order for the breach
to be sufficient for the employee to treat the contract as being discharged the
employer’s conduct must amount to a significant breach going to the heart of
the contract or show that the employer no longer intends to be bound by one or
more of the essential terms of the contract. ( Gibb v JAAR [2018]
TRE 135 and Western Excavating (ECC) v Sharpe [1978] ICR 221). 

 22. It is also well established that a term
of ‘mutual trust and confidence’ is to be implied into all contracts of
employment and there is a two-stage test for determining whether there has been
a breach of this implied term ( Maclagan v States Employment Board JET
79/15 and Malik v BCCI [1997] UKHL 23). The Tribunal must be
satisfied that: 

 (a) there
was conduct which destroyed or seriously damaged the trust between employer and
employee; and 

 (b) the employer's conduct was done without
reasonable and proper cause. 

 23. If the employer acts in an unreasonable
way there may be a breach of mutual trust and confidence if the test in Western
Excavating is satisfied, but mere unreasonable behaviour is not
enough. The behaviour must be ‘seriously unreasonable’ or
‘sufficiently serious’ so as to amount to a breach that enables
the employee to treat the contract as being discharged. 

 24. Any
breach of this implied term is a fundamental breach amounting to a repudiation,
which necessarily goes to the root of the contract and allows the employee to
resign and claim constructive dismissal ( Maclagan and Morrow
v Safeway Stores Ltd [2002] IRLR 9, EAT). However, if the employer’s
conduct is not sufficiently serious then there is no breach of the implied
term. 

 25. In
order to establish constructive dismissal, the ‘range of reasonable responses
test’, which is the test usually used to establish unfair dismissal, is not
appropriate ( Luce v Newtel Limited [2018] TRE 147
and Buckland v Bournemouth University Higher Education Corporation [2010]
IRLR 445). The test to be applied is an objective one; whether or
not the employer intended to breach the contract is irrelevant ( Lewis
v Motorworld Garages Ltd [1986] ICR 157). It is well
established that the conduct of the employer must be looked at objectively in
all the circumstances and from the perspective of a reasonable person in the
position of the innocent party ( Maclagan and Malik ). 

 26. The breach of the implied obligation of
trust and confidence may consist of a series of actions by the employer which,
taken cumulatively, amount to a breach of the term, although
each particular incident may not do so in isolation. The
Tribunal in Maclagan , having determined whether or
not each individual alleged act and omission was a breach, then summarised
that the test for assessing the whole course of conduct is as follows: 

 “The
Tribunal noted that the test in such situations is whether, viewed objectively,
(i.e. from the perspective of a reasonable person in the position of the
innocent party) there was some act or omission (or series of acts or omissions)
which was “likely to destroy or seriously damage the relationship of confidence
and trust between employer and employee… so that the relationship is
fundamentally damaged and not able to continue as before.” ” 

 5. The
parties brought to my attention some additional authorities. Mr Flynn referred
me to the English case of Quilter Private Client Advisers Ltd v
Falconer and another [2022] IRLR 227 at 115. The judgment highlights
that in assessing whether there has been a breach, what is significant is the
impact of the employer’s behaviour on the employee rather than what the
employer intended. The impact is assessed objectively. Mr Flynn also referred
me to the English Court of Appeal case of Wandsworth London Borough
Council v D’Silva [1998] IRLR 193 CA. The case says a provision in a
contract that permits unilateral variation by an employer needs to be exercised
reasonably. 

 6. Mr Child
referred me to the House of Lords judgment in Malik v BCCI [1998]
AC 20 which says at page 45D that the implied term of trust and confidence can
be excluded or modified by the parties. This means that the
express terms of a contract take precedence over the
implied term. The same point is made in Reda v Flag Limited a
decision of the Privy Council dated 11 July 2002. In paragraph 45 their
Lordships confirmed that the implied term of trust and confidence must yield to
the express provisions of the contract. 

 7. In London Borough of Waltham
Forest v Omilaju [2004] EWCA Civ 1493 paragraphs 16 to 22 the Court of
Appeal provided helpful guidance on the last straw
doctrine. Although the final straw may be relatively insignificant,
it must not be utterly trivial (paragraph 16). It must contribute something to
the breach although what it adds may be relatively insignificant (paragraph
19). An entirely innocuous act on the part of the employer cannot be a final
straw even if the employee genuinely interprets it as hurtful and destructive
(paragraph 22). If the final straw is entirely innocuous, the claim fails and
there is no need to consider the impact of the earlier acts. This is because
the employee, by failing to resign in relation to the earlier acts, has
affirmed the contract (paragraph 21). 

 8. In Kaur v Leeds Teaching Hospital NHS
Trust [2018] EWCA Civ 978 at paragraph 43 the Court of Appeal
confirmed that if there is a course of conduct that amounts to a breach of the
implied term and the employee continues to work they will not be held
to have affirmed the contract. This means that if there are further acts the
employee can rely on the totality of the conduct to establish a breach of the
implied term. 

 9. Finally,
Mr Child referred to the Court of Appeal decision in Abraham v
Nottingham City Council [2018] EWCA Civ 796 paragraphs 83 to 85 which
is authority for the point that a unilateral variation to a contract of
employment by an employer can be accepted by the employee’s conduct including
continuing to work without objection. Whether the employee’s conduct amounts to
acceptance will depend on the circumstances of each case.” 

 36. It
is also relevant in the current case to consider the requirements of article 3(2)
of the Employment Law, which sets out the particulars that a written statement
of terms of employment must contain. Those particulars include: 

 “(d) the scale or rate of remuneration or the
method of calculating remuneration; … 

 (f) any terms and conditions relating to
hours of work (including any terms and conditions relating to normal working
hours); 

 (g) any terms and conditions relating
to – 

 (i) entitlement to holidays, including
public holidays, and holiday pay (the particulars given being sufficient to
enable the employee’s entitlement, including any entitlement to accrued holiday
pay on the termination of employment, to be precisely calculated), …” 

 37. Finally, I also
considered Article 77F of the Employment Law which allows
the tribunal to reduce an award for unfair dismissal by such amount as it
considers just and equitable if: 

 “(5) The
Tribunal considers that any conduct of the complainant before dismissal (or,
where the dismissal was with notice, before the notice was given) that
contributed directly to the dismissal was such that reduction of the award is
just and equitable. … 

 (10) Any
circumstances that the Tribunal considers would be just and equitable to take
into account.” 

 38. I n Wolfson
v Trident Trust Company Limited [2014] JRC 228, Sir Michael Birt,
then Bailiff of Jersey, referred to Article 77F(10) as “ a sweep-up provision
which enables [the Tribunal] to take into account any other circumstances which
would render it just and equitable to reduce the award .” 

 Conclusion 

 39. To determine
whether the Claimant was constructively dismissed I considered the questions
set out below. To answer each question, I applied the law referenced above to
my findings on the facts of this case. 

 Was there a fundamental
breach of contract? 

 40. It
is evident from the Claimant’s WhatsApp messages to Mr Turrell on or around 7 February
2025 that the Claimant understood the Respondent no longer intended to be bound
by the 38 Hours Term of his employment contract and he requested clarification
on this point. After Mr Turrell responded, reminding the Claimant of their
recent meeting and saying “ you have your contact ”, the Claimant again requested
further clarification and asked for the terms of his contract in writing. Mr
Turrell did not send him anything following this date. 

 41. While
Ms Terry asserts the Claimant would have been given the updated terms of his
contract in April 2024, the Claimant does not recall this and no direct
evidence that he was given a copy in April 2024 was put forward. Mr Turrell
asserts that he gave the Claimant a “blank” copy of his contract in January
2025, which was either printed off or came from a file and he was unclear on
which of the Claimant’s details were included in it. Conversely, the Claimant
denies receiving this. In addition, no copy of any contract referencing the 38 Hours
Term was included in the File. 

 42. On
a balance of probabilities, based on the responses the witnesses gave when
questioned at the Hearing and the contemporaneous documentation in the File
(including the absence of the relevant contract), I prefer the Claimant’s
evidence that he was not provided with the updated terms of his employment in
writing. 

 43. I
note that the Claimant was requesting further information and a copy of his
contract from Mr Turrell in the context of a change in relation to his holiday
pay, which was paid on the basis of a zero-hour arrangement, and a written communication
from Ms Mason stating that he was on a zero-hour contract. While Mr Turrell and
Ms Mason gave evidence that this was an error and Mr Turrell said he told the Claimant
this, the Claimant denies being told this and I have not been shown any
evidence that this error was corrected in writing at any stage prior to the
Claimant’s resignation. Although the Respondent states the error was corrected
in the Claimant’s February pay, there is no assertion the Claimant’s February
pay statement was provided to him prior to his resignation on 20 February 2025. 

 44. It
would not have been difficult for Mr Turrell to write to the Claimant between 7
and 20 February 2025 to reassure him that he would be offered 38 hours per week
going forward, but he did not do this. In addition, Mr Turrell chose not to
respond to the Claimant’s requests on 13 and 14 February 2025 for information
the Claimant said he needed for Social Security. Mr Turrell gave evidence that
this was a question that should have been put to Ms Mason, yet he did not say
that he told the Claimant this nor that he referred the matter to Ms Mason. It
is understandable that the Claimant felt that the Respondent no longer wished
to engage with him in relation to his employment contract and reached the
conclusion that it no longer agreed to be bound by the 38 Hours Term. 

 45. It
is not clear from the evidence in the File whether it is the Respondent’s
position that the Claimant’s contract included a clause which allowed it to
offer the Claimant less than 38 hours over the winter (a “Winter Hours
Clause”). Both the Response Form and paragraph 6 of Mr Turrell’s statement simply
states that the Claimant “was contracted to work 38 hours per week ”
[19]. While paragraph 18 of Mr Turrell’s statement refers to the Claimant’s “ belief
… that his contract differed from those of other full-time employees,
specifically regarding a winter hour zero-hour clause” and paragraph 19
references the Claimant’s “misunderstanding
regarding the nature of the full-time contract, specifically concerning the
inclusion of ‘winter hours’” , Mr
Turrell does not specify whether it is the Respondent’s position that the
Winter Hours Clause applied to the Claimant or not. However, when asked about
this at the Hearing, Mr Turrell said that all of the Respondent’s employment
contracts, including the Claimant’s, included the Winter Hours Clause. 

 46. The
parties both assert that the Claimant was “ contracted to work 38 hours per
week ”. By this, I understand them to mean that the Respondent agreed to
offer the Claimant 38 hours per week and the Claimant agreed to work that
number of hours. I agree with Advocate Blakely’s submission that the only term
that should be imported into the original signed contract of employment is the
38 Hours Term. Accordingly, I find that there was no Winter Hours Clause agreed
between the parties. 

 47. I
further find that the conduct of the Respondent in January and February 2025,
both in the errors that were made and its failure to respond to the Claimant’s
request for clarification prior to 20 February 2025, represented to the
Claimant that the Respondent no longer agreed to be bound by the 38 Hours Term,
at least not without the Winter Hours Clause also applying. 

 48. I
considered Advocate Blakely’s submission that the Respondent was entitled to offer
the Claimant less than 38 hours per week in accordance with the Shortage of
Work provision in the handbook. However, the first time this was raised was at
the end of the Hearing. The Claimant did not have the opportunity to respond to
this and no evidence was put forward from either party in relation to it’s
applicability. For example, no evidence was given that this was the version of
the handbook that was incorporated into the contract between the parties or whether
a copy was provided to the Claimant at any stage during his employment. I am
also not persuaded that the Shortage of Work provision would be sufficient to
cover the same situation as the Winter Hours Clause. 

 49. Article
3(2) of the Employment Law requires the Respondent to set out in writing,
amongst other things, any terms relating to the Claimant’s hours of work and
entitlement to holidays and holiday pay. Not only have I found that the
Respondent did not do this, but I have found that it failed to do so even after
the Claimant requested clarification of the terms of his employment in writing.
In addition, I have found that the Respondent represented to the Claimant that
it no longer intended to be bound by the 38 Hours Term, thus changing an
essential term of the contract. It was clear the Claimant did not agree to this
and this would, therefore, be a unilateral change to a fundamental term of the contract.

 50. Following
the judgments in the cases of Gibb and Western
Excavating , if the employer’s conduct shows that the employer no longer
intends to be bound by one or more of the essential terms of the contract, this
would constitute a fundamental breach of contract. I have found that the
Respondent’s conduct showed that it no longer intended to be bound by the term
of the contract in relation to the Claimant’s hours of work, which is an
essential term, and accordingly this is a fundamental breach of contract. 

 51. I
find that the Respondent’s conduct seriously damaged the trust between the
parties, amounting to a breach of the term of mutual trust and confidence which
is implied into all employment relationships. In accordance with the cases of Maclagan and Malik ,
I reached this conclusion after considering the Respondent’s actions (and
inactions) from the perspective of a reasonable person in the position of the
Claimant. I have not made a finding as to whether the Respondent intended to
make this representation, but I note that, in accordance with the cases of Lewis 
and Quilter , this is not a significant consideration. 

 52. As
set out in the cases of Malik and Marrow , a breach
of the term of mutual trust and confidence. without any reasonable or proper
cause, is a fundamental breach of contract, which allows the employee to resign
and claim constructive dismissal. Consequently, the Respondent’s conduct
constituted a fundamental breach of contract. 

 Did
the Claimant resign as a result? 

 53. Having
found the Respondent’s conduct constituted a fundamental breach of contract, I went
on to consider whether the Claimant resigned as a result of that conduct. 

 54. The
Respondent has not provided an alternative reason for the Claimant’s
resignation, and I accept that the Claimant resigned because he understood that
the Respondent had unilaterally changed the terms of his contract relating to
hours. This is made clear in the email the Claimant sent notifying the Respondent
of his resignation on 20 February 2025 as well as the Claimant’s witness
evidence. 

 55. Consequently,
I find that the Claimant resigned as a result of the Respondent’s conduct which
constituted a fundamental breach of contract. 

 Did
the Claimant affirm the contract? 

 56. When
determining whether the Claimant did anything to demonstrate that he affirmed
the employment contract following the Respondent’s breach, I considered the
case of Abraham , which made the point that a unilateral variation
to a contract can be accepted by an employee continuing to work without
objection, but whether the employee’s conduct amounts to acceptance will depend
on the circumstances of each case. 

 57. In the current case,
although the Claimant continued to work for a short period, he did not do so
without objection, writing to both Ms Mason and Mr Turrell to object and, the
Respondent asserts, failing to attend work for two days in protest in relation
to his contract and working hours. 

 58. There
is no assertion by the Respondent that the Claimant did anything else to affirm
the breach, he resigned relatively soon after he understood the Respondent
intended not to comply with the 38 Hours Term and shortly after he had requested
clarification and the Respondent failed to provided it. 

 59. Accordingly,
I find that the Claimant did not affirm the fundamental breach of contract
which he resigned as a result of and, consequently, the Claimant was
constructively dismissed. 

 Was the dismissal fair? 

 60. No
fair reason has been given for the dismissal. Accordingly, I find that the
dismissal was unfair and the claim for unfair dismissal succeeds. 

 How
much compensation should the Claimant be awarded? 

 61. As the Claimant had continuous employment of between 1 and 2
years, under article 77F of the Employment Law and the Schedule to the
Employment (Awards) (Jersey) Order 2009, the maximum amount of compensation the
Tribunal can award is 8 weeks’ pay. 

 62. I
have carefully considered whether there is any reason to reduce the award under
Article 77F and, in particular, I note that Mr Turrell suggested in his WhatsApp message on 7
February 2025 that the Claimant arrange a meeting with him on the Claimant’s
return to work to discuss the Claimant’s questions. I considered whether compensation
should be reduced on the basis that the Claimant did not do this prior to
resigning. 

 63. The Claimant
provided contemporaneous written evidence that he asked for his contract in
writing in a situation where the Respondent admits Ms Mason made errors in her
representation to the Claimant of an essential term of that contract and the
Claimant made it clear he was confused by the subsequent discussions he had
with Mr Turrell regarding this. The Respondent provided no persuasive evidence
that the Claimant’s request for clarification was properly dealt with. The
Tribunal was even not provided with a copy of the contract the Respondent’s
witnesses sought to rely on. I am not satisfied that the situation would have
been any different had the Claimant met with Mr Turrell again. Accordingly, I
do not believe that a reduction should be applied on the basis the Claimant did
not wait to meet with him. 

 64. I note that the
Respondent made assertions in relation to the Claimant failing to attend work. However,
I do not consider there to be evidence of sufficiently blameworthy conduct by
the Claimant to make it just and equitable for a reduction in the award on this
basis. Further, I do not consider there to be any other reason which would make
a reduction just and equitable. 

 65. The
Claimant calculates his weekly wage in the claim form as £589, based on £15.50
per hour for 38 hours per week. The Respondent does not dispute this
calculation. Accordingly, 8 weeks’ wages equals £4,712 and the Claimant is
awarded this amount as compensation for unfair dismissal. 

 Unpaid
Wages Claim 

 66. The
basis for the unpaid wages claim is unclear. 

 67. In
the claim form the Claimant calculated this claim on the grounds that on 28
January 2025 he should have been paid 4 week’s wages, which, based on 38 hours
per week at a rate of £15.50 per hour, amounts to £2,556, but he was only paid
£1,946.96. Accordingly, he claimed the difference of £409.04. 

 68. However,
while there is evidence that there was an agreement the Claimant was contracted
to work at least 38 hours work per week, there is no evidence that he should be
paid for 38 hours per week in situations where he was offered this number of
hours but declined to work them. 

 69. In
response to this ground the Respondent asserted that the Claimant was not paid
for working on 21, 22, 27 or 28 January 2025 as he did not work on these dates
and he was absent from work without authorization. At the Hearing, the Claimant
was unclear whether or not he worked on these dates. He thought he may have
been too ill to work on 28 and 29 January 2025, but stated that he did not
notify the Respondent that he was sick on these dates. 

 70. At
the Hearing, the grounds for the Claimant’s unpaid wages claim appeared to be
that, while he was only paid for working 45.5 hours in February 2025, he
thought he had worked closer to 65 hours in February 2025. 

 71. Ms
Mason explained at the Hearing that she calculated that the Claimant worked
45.5 hours in Feburary 2025 based on the times sheets that the Claimant had
submitted. The Claimant did not assert that he had submitted timesheets saying
he worked more hours than this, but he did say his diary may show this. Neither
he nor the Respondent seemed to know exactly where his diary was, but there was
a suggestion that it may be in the Respondent’s yard. The Claimant said he had
asked for it but it was not provided. This was not raised with the Tribunal in
advance of the Hearing and, accordingly, no specific order was made for its
production. 

 72. No
other evidence was provided to show that the Claimant worked for more hours
than he was paid for. Neither was any evidence put forward to show that he
should be paid for any hours, despite not working them. Accordingly, the claim
for unpaid wages fails.

title: BETWEEN WESLEY ROSTRON v DISCRIMINATION TRIBUNAL IN THE MATTER BETWEEN WESLEY ROSTRON CLAIMANT AND SEA LEVEL ECO DISTILLERY LIMITED
text:
IN THE JERSEY EMPLOYMENT
AND DISCRIMINATION TRIBUNAL 

 IN THE
MATTER BETWEEN 

 WESLEY
 ROSTRON 

 CLAIMANT 

 AND 

 SEA
 LEVEL ECO DISTILLERY LIMITED 

 RESPONDENT 

 TRIBUNAL
 JUDGMENT 

 Reference: [2025]
TRE 33 

 Date of Hearing: 24 September 2025 

 Before: Mrs
Hannah Westmacott, Deputy Chairman 

 For the Claimant: In
person 

 For
the Respondent: Ms
Sarah Gaudion 

 DECISION 

 The Claimant’s claim for breach of contract succeeds
and the Respondent shall pay £1,308 to the Claimant by way of damages. The
Claimant is responsible for paying any tax or social security contributions due
on the damages awarded. 

REASONS 

 Introduction 

 1. 
 The Claimant was employed by the Respondent to work as
a Distillery Manager from 7 to 18 January 2025, when he was dismissed with
immediate effect and without payment in lieu of notice. 

 2. 
 On 3 March 2025, the Claimant presented a claim against
the Respondent for breach of contract for failure to give notice of termination
of his employment. The Respondent defends the claim on the grounds that it was
entitled to dismiss the Claimant without notice because he committed gross
misconduct, in that he failed to properly lock the Respondent’s premises when
he left on 17 January 2025. 

 3. 
 A case management meeting was held on the claim at
which a final hearing (the “Hearing”) was listed. In advance of the Hearing,
witness statements were provided to the Tribunal from the Claimant and, on
behalf of the Respondent, Sarah Gaudion. Both parties also provided a small
bundle of documents in addition to their witness statements. Both witnesses
attended the Hearing, where they were questioned on the evidence before the
Tribunal. 

 Evidence 

 4. The Claimant
worked for approximately 6 to 7 days during his employment with the Respondent,
one day was spent at the Respondent’s distillery and the remainder at the
Respondent’s shop (the “Shop”). Ms Gaudion asserts the Claimant was responsible
for locking the Shop every day that he worked there. The Claimant said he
locked up a few times, but that once or twice he left with or before someone
else, who locked the Shop. 

 5. On 17
January 2025, Ms Gaudion and the Claimant were both present in the Shop and
they held a discussion, during which they agreed that the Claimant’s employment
would terminate and the Claimant would work and be paid for his 2 weeks’ notice
period. At the end of his shift, after Ms Gaudion left, the Claimant closed the
Shop. 

 6. There were 2
locks on the Shop door. At the Hearing, the Claimant could not recall the exact
position of the locks; he had not operated them for over 8 months and even then
he only did so a few times. Ms Gaudion, having had the benefit of still using
the locks on a daily basis, explained that one lock was right at the top of the
door and the other was at the bottom, near to the ground. She stated that the
locks were new in, or shortly before, January 2025 and both operated well and
easily. 

 7. The Claimant
was certain he locked both locks when he closed the Shop on 17 January 2025. At
the Hearing, he explained that he was very safety conscious and he made sure he
properly locked the door, checking the handle and looking through the window to
check that everything was switched off inside the Shop. He recalled that even
after he started walking to his car he turned back to check the handle again. He
said that he was particularly careful because locking the Shop was new to him. He
had been a key holder in many of his prior roles and had always locked up properly. 

 8. However, Ms
Gaudion asserts that, when she went to open the Shop on 18 January 2025, she bent
down to open the bottom lock then, as she stood up, she put her hand on the
handle and the door opened, without her having to undo the top lock.
Consequently, she asserts the top lock was not locked by the Claimant the day
before. 

 9. Ms Gaudion said
she had planned to speak to the Claimant about this when he arrived at work at
11am, but he did not turn up at this time. Ms Gaudion, therefore, called the
Claimant at 11.11am, who said he did not realise his shift started at 11am,
thinking it started at midday as it had the previous day. Ms Gaudion told the
Claimant not to come in. The call lasted 28 seconds and Ms Gaudion did not
mention the door not being properly locked on this call. 

 10. Ms Gaudion
then emailed the Claimant at 3.20pm stating: 

 “Following
a no show for your shift today, on top of that the fact that the shop door was
not locked last night, your employment has been terminated with immediate
effect.” 

 11. 
 The Claimant responded to this email at 5.43pm stating: 

 “I can
confirm that I always ensure the door is properly locked before leaving. If
there was an issue with the door being left unlocked, I am unsure how that may
have occurred.” and “…I had checked it 3 times
before leaving...” 

 12. 
 Ms Gaudion wrote to the Claimant again on 20 January
2025 at 11.01am stating: 

 “I know
the door wasn’t locked as I opened up on Saturday morning. The bottom lock had
been locked but not the top lock…” 

 13. 
 Again, the
Claimant responded the same day at 10.06pm stating: 

 “I am
still confident that I securely locked the door properly on the Friday night
(both locks).” 

 14. The
Respondent has provided a copy of the Disciplinary Procedure pages of its handbook,
which gives examples of gross misconduct and specifies the following as one: 

 “Failing
to secure the property during closing hours or any action that invalidates our
insurance.” 

 15. 
 The handbook also goes on to state that: 

 “Gross
Misconduct may lead to immediate suspension or dismissal without prior warning,
depending on the severity of the offense.” 

 16. The handbook
is not referred to in the contract of employment between the parties. At the
hearing, Ms Gaudion said that she did not provide the Claimant with a copy of
the handbook, but that it was on the Respondent’s electronic document system,
which the Claimant had access to. She noted that one of the tasks that she had
discussed with the Claimant during a meeting on 7 January 2025 was that, as
distillery manager with responsibility for other staff, he should review the
staff handbook to check that it was appropriate. She provided a copy of a page
from a notebook that she left in the Shop for the Claimant, on which she had
written “ Review handbook Lizzie done ”. She asserted she wrote this
following their meeting on 7 January 2025 and that the Claimant could have
looked for the handbook himself on the document system or asked where to find
it. 

 17. The Claimant
said he was never provided with a copy of the handbook. He said he was aware of
the electronic document system and that he could access it on the Respondent’s
computer, but he was not or told where to find any particular documents on the
system. 

 Conclusion 

 18. The Claimant
does not have sufficient length of service to make a claim for unfair
dismissal. Accordingly, the tribunal does not need to make a determination as
to the fairness of the dismissal. The relevant question is not what was in the
mind of the person dismissing the Claimant, as it would be when determining
whether a dismissal is fair. Instead, the tribunal must determine whether the
Claimant actually committed gross misconduct, sufficient to entitle the
Respondent to dismiss him without notice. 

 Was the Door Locked? 

 19. The Respondent
asserts the gross misconduct was the Claimant’s failure to properly lock the
Shop door. I, therefore, first considered whether the Claimant properly locked the
Shop door. 

 20. Within a few
days of the Claimant closing the Shop and Ms Gaudion opening it, they both sent
emails, respectively, saying that the Claimant locked both locks and that Ms
Gaudion found the top lock unlocked the next day. Accordingly, I do not find
that either witness is misremembering whether the top lock was locked or not. Upon
considering the oral evidence at the Hearing, I also do not believe that either
party was intentionally seeking to deceive me, and I cannot say that I believed
one witness more than the other on this point. 

 21. Accordingly,
I considered whether either the Claimant or Ms Gaudion could have been
mistaken. Given Ms Gaudion’s explanation that she did not even have to put her
key into the top lock before reaslising it was not locked, I think it is
unlikely she was mistaken. It is possible that the Claimant put his key in the
top lock and removed it again, thinking it was locked, but without actually
locking it. He said he checked the handle after locking up, but Ms Gaudion gave
evidence that the handle would not open the door even if only one lock was
locked. However, Ms Gaudion also gave evidence that the lock was new and easy
to operate, so it is also unlikely that the Claimant made a mistake. 

 22. Consequently,
I considered whether there were any other possibilities, for example, could something
have happened to the top lock in between the Claimant closing the Shop and Ms
Gaudion opening it. No evidence was put forward on this point and it does not
appear to have been something the parties investigated. There may have been
other keyholders and Ms Gaudion gave evidence that, at the time, the alarm and
CCTV systems were not in operation. Accordingly, it is possible the top lock
could have been tampered with or used by someone else in the interim. 

 23. Based on the
evidence before me, I cannot say which possibility is more likely. However, it
is for the Respondent to prove, on a balance of probabilities, that the
Claimant committed the act it asserts constitutes gross misconduct, and I am
not satisfied that the Respondent has done this. Accordingly, the Claimant’s
claim for failure to give notice succeeds on this basis. 

 24. However, in
case I am wrong, and the Claimant mistakenly only locked one of the two locks,
I considered whether this would constitute gross misconduct entitling the
Respondent to dismiss him without notice. 

 What Constitutes Gross Misconduct? 

 25. The Royal
Court, at paragraph 165 of its judgment in the case of Pender v GGH (Jersey) Limited and Ors [2023]JRC124 ,
noted that there is no statutory definition of gross misconduct in either
Jersey or England and Wales. This was followed by a reference to the UK
judgment in the case of Neary v. Dean of Westminster [1999] IRLR
288, which defined gross misconduct as conduct that “ so undermines the
relationship of trust and confidence... that [the employer] should no longer be
required to retain [the employee] in his employment ”. The Royal Court found
that this definition made it clear that what is being considered is exceptionally
serious misconduct such as to merit summary dismissal. 

 26. Following
this, to determine whether conduct constitutes gross misconduct, it is relevant
to consider how damaging the conduct is to the relationship between the
parties. It is uncontroversial to say that dishonesty and other deliberate
actions can damage relationships, but in some cases considerable negligence, which
may consist of a repeated or inexcusable failure to comply with reasonable
instructions, may also be significantly damaging. 

 27. The
Deputy Chairman of the Tribunal at paragraph 4.15 of his judgment in the case
of Jeanette Mash v States Employment Board [2023] TRE 53 
stated that, “in terms of gross misconduct, the Tribunal should consider
“both the character of the conduct and whether it was reasonable for the Trust
[employer] to regard the conduct as having the character of gross misconduct on
the facts” ( Sandwell & West Birmingham Hospitals NHS Trust v
Westwood UKEAT/0032/09).” 

 28. Failing to
lock up a property could be considered gross misconduct in some circumstances,
in particular, if it is done deliberately or recklessly. In this case, there is
no assertion that it was done deliberately and, based on the considerations set
out above, I am not satisfied that the Claimant’s actions were reckless. I am,
therefore, considering only whether, if one of two locks was mistakenly left
unlocked and the door, having been checked, did not open, this would constitute
gross misconduct. 

 29. In my
judgment it is not reasonable to consider that such conduct so undermined the
relationship of trust and confidence between the parties that the Respondent
was entitled to dismiss the Claimant with immediate effect. The conduct was not
repeated and neither was it, in my view, inexcusable. 

 30. I note that
Ms Gaudion also referenced other issues she had with the Claimant’s conduct,
although she does not assert that these constitute gross misconduct. However,
even considering the other conduct issues together with mistakenly failing to lock
one of the two locks, I still do not consider that it is reasonable to regard
the Claimant’s conduct as damaging the relationship between the parties to such
an extent so as to justify summary dismissal. 

 31. Before
finally concluding on this point, I considered the Respondent’s reference to
the examples of gross misconduct set out in its handbook, which includes a failure
to secure the Shop when closed and anything which invalidates the Respondent’s
insurance, which the Respondent asserts only locking one lock would do. 

 32. I note that,
if the contract between the parties lists examples of gross misconduct,
although not necessarily determinative, this may assist in justifying dismissal
without notice on the grounds of the misconduct. 

 33. However, I
do not find that the handbook was incorporated into the contract between the
parties. There is no reference to the handbook in the contract and even on the
Respondent’s evidence, the Claimant was not provided with a finalized copy of
the handbook. While Ms Gaudion asserts she gave the Claimant the task of
checking whether the handbook (which he was to find for himself on the
electronic document system) was appropriate as part of his role in managing
other staff, this is not the same as incorporating the terms of the handbook into
the Claimant’s contract. 

 34. Accordingly,
in the circumstances of this case, I do not find that the conduct of the
Claimant constitutes gross misconduct. The Respondent’s failure to give the
Claimant notice of his dismissal is, therefore, in breach of the contract
between the parties and the Claimant is entitled to damages in respect of any
loss suffered because of that breach. 

 Award 

 35. 
 The parties agree the Claimant was entitled to two
weeks’ notice of dismissal. 

 36. 
 The Claimant has provided several emails and letters
demonstrating that he sought alternative employment during his two weeks’
notice period, and gave evidence that he was not able to secure alternative
employment until after it expired. Accordingly, he has demonstrated that he
sought to mitigate his loss but was unable to so do and is, therefore, awarded
the full two weeks’ pay in lieu of notice. 

 37. The parties
agreed at the Hearing that the Claimant weekly wage was £654, based on an
annual salary of £34,000 divided by 52. Accordingly, had the Claimant worked
for his two weeks’ notice period, he would have been paid £1,308. 

 38. The
Respondent is, therefore, ordered to pay the Claimant damages of £1,308. The Claimant is responsible for paying any tax or
social security contributions due on the damages
awarded. 

 Mrs
H Westmacott, Deputy Chairman Sent to the parties on: 6 October 2025

title: [2025] TRE 39
text:
IN THE JERSEY EMPLOYMENT AND
DISCRIMINATION TRIBUNAL 

 BETWEEN 

 FABIO GOMES 

 CLAIMANT 

 AND 

 FABIO FERREIRA T/A 1 ST CLASS
 REMOVALS 

 RESPONDENT 

 JUDGMENT 

 Reference: [2025] TRE 39 

 Hearing
date: 31
July 2025 

 Before:
 Advocate
Dr Elena Moran, Chairman 

 For
the Claimant : In person 

 For
the Respondent: In
person 

 DECISION 

 The Respondent must pay the Claimant £1,496 as
compensation for no contract and no payslips. The claims for unfair dismissal
and notice pay fail. 

 REASONS 

 1. 
 Fabio Gomes worked Fabio Ferreira as a removals man. He
started work in May 2024. He says he was dismissed without notice on 1 February
2025. Mr Gomes was paid £17 an hour and generally worked 40 hours a week. On 12
March 2025 he submitted a Claim Form claiming compensation for no contract and
no payslips, unfair dismissal and notice pay. 

 2. Mr Gomes and Mr
Ferreira gave evidence. Mr Ferreira’s bookeeper 
Pamela Smith gave evidence on his behalf as did another member of staff called
Miguel Sousa. I was also provided with copies of Facebook messages, text
messages, bank statements and joint employer returns. 

 Contract 

 3. Article 3(1) of
the Employment (Jersey) Law 2003 says that an employer must give an employee a
written statement of the terms of employment within four weeks of beginning
employment. The Tribunal can award up to eight weeks pay for a breach of
Article 3(1). 

 4. Mr Ferreira admits
that Mr Gomes was never given a written contract. He says that they agreed oral
terms. He asked Mr Gomes if he wanted a written contract and he said it wasn’t
necessary as he cannot read or write. Mr Gomes says that he doesn’t remember
any discussions about a contract. Ms Smith said that she assists Mr Ferreira
with the employer returns and other administration matters. Around July/August
2024 she was preparing a zero hour contract for Mr
Gomes. Mr Ferreira told her in a message that Mr Gomes said he did not want a
contract because he could not read or write. Ms Smith spoke to someone at
Revenue Jersey who told her that in the absence of a written contract a verbal
contract must be agreed within two weeks of employment. Ms Smith pointed out
that Mr Ferreira was setting up a new business and was keen to make sure that
everything was done properly. She says a complaint has been lodged with Revenue
Jersey about the incorrect advice given. 

 5. I find that there
were discussions between Mr Ferreira and Mr Gomes and Mr Gomes said he did not
require a contract. Ms Smith checked the position with Revenue Jersey and was
given incorrect advice. Asked what detriment he had suffered from not having a
contract he said he might not have been sacked in the way he was. 

 6. I consider that Mr
Ferreira has a reasonable excuse for the breach of Article 3(1) as he was given
incorrect information by Revenue Jersey. In addition, Mr Gomes expressly said
that he did not require a contract. Applying the Tribunal Guidance on compensation
for no contract I consider this to be a case where nominal compensation is
warranted. I award Mr Gomes compensation of £136. The sum is to be paid gross
without any deduction for income tax or social security. 

 Payslips 

 7. Article 51 of the Employment
Law says that an employer must give an employee a written itemised pay
statement at or before the time at which wages are paid. The Tribunal can award
up to eight weeks’ gross pay as compensation for no pay slips. 

 8. Mr Ferreira paid
his staff weekly on the last working day of the week which was a Friday or a
Saturday. He worked out how much Mr Gomes was owed and deducted tax and social
security. Sometimes he would round up the amount to pay him a bit extra. No pay
slips were prepared by Mr Ferreira. He provided information about gross pay and
ITIS rate to Ms Smith, and she filed the joint return. 

 9. Mr Gomes’ bank
statements show weekly payments into his bank account plus other payments.
These were loans from Mr Ferreira to Mr Gomes which were generally repaid by
deductions from wages. There were also bonus payments. 

 10. Mr Ferreira said
that Mr Gomes did not want payslips. Mr Gomes says he does not remember
discussing pay slips or asking for a pay slip. 

 11. Mr Ferreira has no
good excuse for not providing pay slips. However, he is a very small employer
and has never employed more than two staff including Mr Gomes. At the time no
procedure was in place to provide pay slips to Mr Gomes although I accept Mr
Ferreira’s evidence that Mr Gomes said he did not want them. Mr Gomes has not
suggested the lack of pay slips caused him any harm. 

 12. Applying the
Tribunal Guidance I consider this to be a moderately serious breach and award
Mr Gomes compensation equal to two weeks’ gross pay in the sum of £1,360. As
this is compensation no income tax or social security is payable. 

 Unfair
Dismissal 

 13. Mr Gomes says he
was summarily dismissed by Mr Ferreira in a telephone call on 1 February 2025.
The background to the call is as follows: - 

 a. As evidenced by a
sick note attached to a Facebook message Mr Gomes became ill on Sunday 19
January 2025. 

 b. The sick note says
that Mr Gomes is signed off work from Monday 20 January 2025. I therefore find
that Mr Gomes did not work on Monday 20 or Tuesday 21 January 2025. 

 c. On Wednesday 22
January 2025 Mr Gomes sent a text message to Mr Ferreira saying, “No sorry
I’m not coming into work I’ll give you a shout tomorrow to let you know what’s
going on. I don’t feel very well.” 

 d. On the same day Mr
Gomes went to see the doctor who gave him a medical certificate for back pain with
a return-to-work date of 3 February 2025. 

 e. At 18:19 on Friday
24 January 2025 Mr Gomes tried to send photos of the front and back of the
medical certificate to Mr Ferreira by text message. The message was not
delivered. 

 f. 
 On Monday 27 January 2025 Mr Gomes sent the photos of
the certificate to Mr Ferreria via Facebook. Mr Ferreira responded with a
thumbs up. 

 g. At the same time Mr
Gomes sent a copy of an appointment card showing his next appointment with the
doctor on Tuesday 28 January 2025. Mr Gomes says that at that appointment the
doctor said he was fine. 

 14. On Saturday 1
February 2025 there was a phone call between Mr Gomes and Mr Ferreira. Mr Gomes
says that he rang because his sick note was running out and he wanted to know
where to meet for work on Monday. Mr Ferreira asked him why he had taken time
off and he explained he had pulled a muscle in his back. There was then a
discussion about the way in which Mr Gomes had been treated by Mr Ferreira. Mr
Ferreira said there was no point in him coming back. Mr Gomes took this to be a
dismissal. He says he went back to the doctor the following week and was signed
off work again. 

 15. Mr Ferreira’s
version of the call is that he got into an argument with Mr Gomes with Mr Gomes
complaining that Mr Sousa was paid the same hourly rate as him. Mr Gomes told
him he had been signed off work for another month. Mr Gomes hung up on him. Mr
Ferreira rang back, and Mr Gomes hung up on him again. He denies sacking Mr
Gomes in the call. He said he genuinely needed him. 

 16. At 10:27 pm on 1
February 2025 Mr Gomes sent a text message to Mr Ferreira. The message starts
by complaining about Mr Sousa being paid the same as him despite having less
experience. He complains in an abusive way about Mr Ferreira’s treatment of him
but does not explicitly say in the message that he has been dismissed. There is
no further communication until another message from Mr Gomes to Mr Ferreira on
Friday 14 February 2025 at 12:46 pm which is much more conciliatory in tone. He
suggests a face-to-face conversation. Again, there is no reference to a
dismissal. Mr Ferreira does not respond to the message. 

 17. The burden is on
Mr Gomes to prove that he was dismissed. I find that he has not discharged that
burden. There is nothing in the text message sent a few hours after the phone
call to suggest that he has been dismissed. Had Mr Ferreria dismissed Mr Gomes
I would have expected Mr Gomes to refer to that in the text. I find that Mr
Ferreira did not say anything on the call that could be reasonably construed as
an immediate dismissal. There is also no reference to a dismissal in the
message sent on 14 February 2025. As Mr Gomes has not established that he was
dismissed his claim of unfair dismissal fails. 

 Notice pay 

 18. Under Article 56
of the Employment Law Mr Gomes was entitled to one week’s notice. As he was not
dismissed his claim for notice pay fails. Even had he been dismissed, on his
own evidence he was signed off sick the following week. If was too ill to work the
lack of notice caused him no loss so his claim for notice pay would fail in any
event. 

 Conclusion 

 19. 
 Mr Ferreira must pay Mr Gomes compensation in the sum
of £1,496. The money must be paid within 14 days of the date of this judgment. 

 Advocate Dr Elena Moran Date:
1 August 2025 

 Compensation for no contract 

 £136 

 Compensation for no pay slips 

 £1,360 

 Total compensation 

 £1,496 

 Page Last Updated: 07 Aug 2025
